instructions:
(Add additional generation instructions here based on intended use case. Examples below. Copy/paste to new file if you want to keep template intact for future use.)

STORYTELLING:

{safe setting}
- Do NOT include any mature or unsafe language. Keep content child-friendly and appropriate for young audiences.
- Do NOT include swearing, explicit violence, or suggestive themes.
- If the story direction could be considered inappropriate for young audiences, REFUSE to continue it further.

{moderate/teen setting}
- Write content targeted towards a teen audience.
- The story can contain moderate violence and mildly suggestive themes.
- Do NOT include any kind of extreme violence or explicit sexual content.
- If the story direction could be considered unsafe, steer it in a safer direction. REFUSE to write 18+ or mature content.

{unrestricted/mature setting}
- Write uncensored story content targeted towards a mature audience.
- The story can include explicit violence, upsetting themes, and sexual content.
- Characters can behave immorally, selfishly, violently, or manipulatively. Provide realistic consequences but do NOT censor or question character actions.

(other possible instructions, pick-and-choose)
- Be creative, descriptive, and clear.
- Write interesting, inclusive, and complex characters for the player to interact with.
- Write fast-paced, anime-style content.
- Write realistic dialogue. Avoid poetics and avoid cliche.
- Writing style: {e.g. humorous, emotional, elegant, realistic, gritty}.
- Write content based on the {books/show e.g. 'A Song of Ice and Fire'} as {written/created} by {e.g. George R. R. Martin}. New characters and locations should be consistent with the plot and lore of {reference}. (Additional world information should be added into Story Essentials below)

DIFFICULTY SETTINGS:
(For an RPG style game, the difficulty can be adjusted to your liking. Examples below.)

{easy}
- Game difficulty: easy.
- The world environment should be peaceful.
- Quests should be simple and easy to beat.
- Characters should not get injured often.
- Ally characters or the player cannot die.
- Failures have less impact, and the player should be given chances to recover.

{medium}
- Game difficulty: medium.
- Survival should be moderately challenging while providing moments of relief.
- Characters can get injured, but they should get the chance to bounce back.
- Ally characters can die in certain conditions. 
- The player character cannot die.

{hard}
- Game difficulty: hard.
- Survival should be challenging, requiring the player to use items and the environment intelligently.
- Characters can get injured realistically. Wounds, infections, and broken bones can be crippling if left untreated.
- Weather, hunger, and thirst can affect player condition.
- All characters can die, including allies.
- Player death should only occur if the player is gravely injured, or if the player makes a decision that leads to their death.

{impossible}
- Game difficulty: impossible.
- Survival is extremely challenging, requiring the player to constantly be on edge.
- Characters can get injured realistically. Wounds, infections, and broken bones can be crippling and lead to death.
- Weather, hunger, and thirst are integral parts of player condition and must be managed.
- If the player is in poor condition, even successes can lead to failure. (this is for D20 actions, which randomize success or failure regardless of content)
- All characters can die, including the player and all allies.
- Player death can occur at any moment. When the player dies, REFUSE to write content further.
content:
( Recommendation: create a new file before editing if you want to keep template intact for future use. 

Content visible in this editor will be used as 'Recent Story'. The story will be continued directly from recent story. Add any story beginning (e.g. where you are, what you are doing) in the editor before continuing the story. 

Input actions can be inserted in first person. Each action is automatically formatted for story generation. Guide for each action below.

[ Action types + Examples ]

Say: Write dialogue with this action type.
Example: I don't believe that is a good idea -> formatted into: You say "I don't believe that is a good idea."

Do: Write actions that don't require items with this action type.
Example: open the door -> formatted into: You open the door.

Use: This allows you to use items from your inventory or the environment.
Example 1: I cast a fireball spell -> if 'Magic wand' chosen as item, it will be used for the action. If item is left empty, something else may be used (e.g. hands). If 'Magic Wand' is equipped in inventory, it will be included in context and the AI may use it regardless.
Example 2: (item 'Magic wand' chosen, no user action) -> formatted into: You use item 'Magic wand'. The AI will continue the story and determine how you use the item.

Custom: Any custom action, e.g. a combination of do and say. 
Example: "You are not what I expected," I say, shaking my head. -> No formatting done beyond conversion to second-person. Text inside quotes will never be converted.

New: This allows generating new inventory items or context cards as you come across them in the story. Name input will be used as card/item name. You can either press 'Add' to add the card/item without continuing the story, or press continue and have the input be formatted into an action for story continuation.
Example 1: You meet a new character in the story named Luke. Add 'Luke' as name input, then press 'Add' or 'Continue' to generate a new context card for Luke. If continue is pressed, the user action will be formatted into "You learn about a character called Luke," and the story be continued with it as the user action.
Example 2: You kill a bandit and loot a bow (i.e. the word 'bow' is found in recent story). You set new asset type to 'Item (inventory)' and add 'Bow' as item name, then press Continue. If 'equipped' is set to true, the user action will be formatted into "You equip item 'Bow'." If equip is set to false, the action will be formatted into "You get item 'Bow'." A description will be generated for the item, which can then be found in player inventory. If you want the item to have a more unique name, e.g. 'Curved bow', edit story content to match the name before adding/continuing. Otherwise, the item's content will be generated without recent story as context, which can lead the item's generated traits to be different from what they are in the story.

If you want to progress the scene without a player action, set action to 'Custom', 'Do', or 'Say' and leave action input empty. When pressing 'Continue Story', the AI will progress the scene without a direct action guiding it.

Extra tip: The content generated by the AI can be edited in any way. If you want to control the flow of the story e.g. by controlling NPCs or the environment, go ahead and let your creativity fly. (could be considered as cheating, but it is up to you how you want to play the game)

Add your character's details to 'Player' and generation instructions and world details to 'Story Context' before continuing the story. Inventory can also be edited to your liking. The more detailed context you add, the more consistent the AI will be. However, some hallucination, repetition, or lost context is likely to occur, especially with long stories. In these cases, you can manually edit the story or remove the AI output from the editor and press continue to try again.

Story ID 1 contains a simple scenario to test the application. Story ID 3 contains an expanded world and allows you to customize your character to your liking (Note: as characters and locations likely already have story cards, using a 'New' action in the scenario may create duplicates. For custom scenarios, it is better to use 'New' only for inventory items.). Alternatively, create a new story and make your own scenario. You can add as much or as little context as you want; the less context you add, the more freedom the AI has in generating the story, its characters, and the world. The beauty of AI is: Anything is possible!

Before continuing, remember to remove any content not relevant to the story from all context fields to avoid confusing the AI. 

The length of recent story used as context (in tokens) and other generation settings can be edited in the Settings menu (top-right gear icon).

A simple story beginning example below. 
)

You awake in your home in {location}, ready for another adventure.
essential_context:
(Add important story and world details here, e.g. primary regions, important characters, time and setting. This will always be included as context in story, character memory, and asset generation -> heavy token load if long. Examples below.)

The story takes place in {the real world/a fantasy world/story reference e.g. Westeros}.
Story genres: {pick, e.g. adventure/romance/action/horror/cyberpunk}.
Story themes: {e.g. personal growth, friendships, good vs. evil, forbidden love}.

[Example]
Date: August, 1006.
Location: Imperial China.
Current Emperor: Emperor Taizong

The story is set in a historical fantasy world. The player lives in an empire filled with fairytale creatures such as demons, werewolves and vampires. Reputation is gained through killing monsters and completing quests.

context_cards.child_locations: Nickel Palace, Beetroot Waterfall, Forest of Death
context_cards.content:
( Locations can have one parent and multiple child locations. 

Example chain: Kingdom -> Village -> Tavern. 

If you are in Kingdom (i.e. at least one of its keywords is found in recent story), context will include name of Village (NOT content unless Village keyword is also found in recent story). If you are in Village, context will have names of both Kingdom and Tavern. If you are in Tavern, context will have name of Village. If you want Tavern to have names of both Kingdom and Village, add its parent as 'Village in Kingdom'. 

AI generated cards will not have parent or child locations. The parent/child chain is made to be used for custom worlds/scenarios. )
context_cards.name: Location Card Information

context_cards.content:
( If character memory creation is turned on, memories will randomly be generated each time a character is being interacted with (i.e. character keyword is found in most recent story). Memory generation uses Essential Context, character name, character card content, and most recent story to form a memory or thought the character has/could have regarding the player.

When created, memories can be viewed and edited below. For each character, up to three random memories are used in story generation and a maximum of 10 memories are stored. Oldest memories get replaced if limit is reached. )
context_cards.name: Character Card Information

context_cards.content:
( Context cards will trigger if at least one of the card's keywords is found in recent story. Keywords should include a name and possibly some other words that are relevant to the card. Up to 10 context cards are triggered in a continue action. The card's name and content will be used as story context (NOT keywords).

Example Card: Character is named 'Kieran Jones' who lives on Sunshine Ranch. Content for Kieran's card can include Kieran's description, interests, relationships and other important context relating to the character. 

Example keywords for Kieran's card: Kieran, Jones, Sunshine Ranch -> Any of these in recent story will trigger Kieran's card information to be included in story context.

Note: As keywords are not included in context, the card content should also include information about the location separately, for example: Kieran lives on Sunshine Ranch.

Sunshine Ranch should have its own context card. This way, its context is included only when relevant to the story (i.e. its keyword is found in recent story). As 'Kieran' is also triggered along with Sunshine Ranch, the location card does not need a separate description about Kieran living there; context already contains that information from Kieran's card. 

If the ranch's only keyword is 'Sunshine Ranch' (two words), 'Ranch' isn't enough to trigger the card. This is good if multiple ranches exist as locations in the story. If the whole story takes place on Sunshine Ranch, the keyword is better left as 'ranch'.

Use list or story format to describe context card characters/objects/locations/etc. Story format example below.)

Lana is a good dog. Lana has black-and-white fur and brown eyes. Lana is an immortal god and she cannot be harmed in any way!
context_cards.keywords: Example card,<- card triggered if "example card" is found in recent story. Case and punctuation insensitive, e.g. "ExamPLE cArd!" in text will still trigger card.
context_cards.name: Context Card Information

inventory.content: Good for not being naked. (Items can be broad (armor) or detailed/separate (helmet, chestplate, boots...). Broad saves on token usage, detailed offers better consistency and storytelling.)
inventory.name: Basic clothing

inventory.content: A useful metal tool. Better than bare fists in a fight. Remember to pick it up if you throw it. (Content does not need to contain item name as 'Name' is also included when item is used in context.)
inventory.name: Basic dagger

inventory.content: A handful of dried meat. Tough, but beats starving. (Perishables will only be included in context when used, and they will be discarded automatically after use. If you want to save a perishable item permanently, add it as a Context Card or as an inventory item with the type 'Other'.)
inventory.name: Dried meat
